Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status

Consciousness Science

Anil Seth

b. 1972

The predictive brain and consciousness as controlled hallucination of the body.

Anil Seth’s view is best understood as an empirically disciplined philosophy of mind built from neuroscience, rather than as a purely armchair metaphysics. Its central thesis is that consciousness is a biologically grounded, embodied, predictive, self-regulatory process. Conscious experience is not a Cartesian immaterial substance, not merely a computational function, not simply “intelligence,” and not a universal property of matter. It is the way a living organism, through a brain embedded in and regulating a body, constructs a world and a self by predicting and controlling the causes of sensory signals.

A compressed formulation would be: consciousness is controlled hallucination in the service of biological self-regulation. The “hallucination” part refers to the predictive, constructive character of perception; the “controlled” part refers to constraint by the world and the body; the “biological self-regulation” part is what separates Seth’s mature position from a generic predictive-processing view or from computational functionalism. In his recent work, especially on artificial consciousness, this biological dimension has become more explicit: Seth argues that consciousness may depend on our nature as living organisms, and that artificial consciousness is unlikely along current AI trajectories unless artificial systems become much more brain-like or life-like.

What follows is a reconstruction of Seth’s philosophy of mind from his scientific papers, essays, and public theoretical commitments. It treats his position historically: as a post-Chalmers, post-classical-computational, embodied predictive-processing naturalism.

1. The problem Seth is trying to transform

The late twentieth-century philosophy of mind was decisively shaped by the distinction between functional explanation and phenomenal experience. Thomas Nagel’s famous criterion says that a being is conscious when there is “something it is like” to be that being; David Chalmers later named the “hard problem” as the problem of explaining why any physical state should be conscious at all rather than merely functional, behavioral, or structural.

Seth’s work accepts the seriousness of subjective experience but resists letting the hard problem define the whole research program. He does not simply deny consciousness, nor does he treat it as an illusion in the strong eliminativist sense. His proposal is to focus on what he calls the real problem: explaining the various properties of consciousness in terms of biological mechanisms, without pretending consciousness does not exist and without beginning from the demand to explain why consciousness exists at all. In that formulation, the target is not “why is there experience rather than no experience?” but “why does this experience have these properties, and how do those properties map onto mechanisms in the brain and body?”

This is a crucial historical move. The old mind–body problem asked how mind and matter relate. Chalmers sharpened this into the explanatory gap between physical structure/function and phenomenal “what-it-is-like-ness.” Seth’s strategy is neither traditional reductionism nor dualism. It is closer to a biological research program: distinguish aspects of the phenomenon, measure them where possible, manipulate them experimentally, and build mechanistic bridges between phenomenology and physiology. Seth explicitly compares this to the scientific treatment of life: rather than solve “life” by metaphysical definition, biologists explained metabolism, homeostasis, reproduction, development, and regulation.

This makes Seth historically important because he shifts the field from a metaphysical impasse to a research architecture. He does not show that the hard problem is logically dissolved. A Chalmersian could still object that all the mechanisms Seth identifies leave open the further question of why they are accompanied by experience. But Seth’s answer is methodological: the way to make progress is to explain, predict, and control the structured properties of experience. If this program succeeds deeply enough, the sense of a remaining metaphysical gap may weaken, just as the mystery of life weakened when the mechanisms of living systems became intelligible.

2. Consciousness is not one explanatory target

A major element of Seth’s thought is anti-monolithic. Consciousness is not a single simple property waiting for one master equation. It is a multidimensional phenomenon. In the “real problem” essay, Seth begins by distinguishing conscious level, conscious content, and conscious self.

Conscious level concerns being conscious at all: the difference between wakefulness, dreaming, anesthesia, coma, vegetative states, minimally conscious states, and so on. Here Seth is interested in measures of brain complexity, integration, causal density, and related dynamical features. His early work with Edelman and others argued that some aspects of consciousness are quantifiable but that no single measure captures the multidimensional complexity of conscious neural systems; a satisfactory theory must combine quantitative and qualitative elements.

Conscious content concerns what one is conscious of: a red apple, a melody, pain, anxiety, time passing, a face, a remembered scene. Here Seth emphasizes predictive processing: perceptual content is not passively read off from sensory input but actively inferred by the brain as its best explanation of ambiguous sensory signals.

Conscious self concerns the experience of being a subject: being a body, owning a body, perceiving from a perspective, intending, acting, remembering oneself over time, and being socially recognized. Seth treats selfhood not as a metaphysical ego but as a family of self-related perceptual constructions. He distinguishes bodily, perspectival, volitional, narrative, and social aspects of selfhood, and he takes bodily/interoceptive selfhood to be especially fundamental.

This pluralism places Seth in tension with single-principle theories of consciousness. In a 2022 review with Tim Bayne, he discusses higher-order theories, global workspace theories, re-entry and predictive-processing theories, and integrated information theory as major contemporary approaches. But in another paper with Jakob Hohwy, Seth argues that progress is better served by “theories for consciousness science” than by monolithic “theories of consciousness”: that is, by frameworks that map physical and biological mechanisms onto both functional and phenomenological properties.

The philosophical consequence is that Seth’s view is not “consciousness = X,” where X is global availability, integrated information, higher-order representation, or prediction error minimization. Rather, consciousness science should build systematic correspondences between structured experience and structured biological processes. This is a form of explanatory pluralism, but not relativism: theories are judged by how well they explain, predict, measure, and manipulate experience.

3. From neural correlates to explanatory mechanisms

Modern consciousness science often began with the search for neural correlates of consciousness: the minimal neural mechanisms sufficient for a conscious percept. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that many neuroscientists set aside the hard problem and focus instead on identifying neural properties that turn consciousness on or off and neural bases of conscious contents. It also emphasizes that correlates are only a first step; they are not necessarily explanatory.

Seth’s work belongs to this neural-correlates tradition but tries to push beyond mere correlation. His publication history shows a long concern with measurement, causal analysis, explanatory correlates, animal consciousness, artificial consciousness, and clinical states of consciousness, including early work on criteria for consciousness in mammals, hallmarks of consciousness in non-mammalian species, the functional utility of conscious contents, measures of consciousness, and explanatory correlates.

The move from correlation to explanation is essential. If a posterior cortical region lights up when someone reports seeing a face, that is not yet an explanation of why face-experience has its particular character. Seth argues that we need a general theory of perception: not just where consciousness happens, but what brains are doing when they generate conscious contents.

That theory, for Seth, is predictive processing broadly construed. The brain is not a camera, and perception is not bottom-up registration. The brain is a hierarchical prediction system that infers the causes of sensory inputs. Sensory signals are ambiguous and noisy; perceptual experience is the brain’s best current hypothesis about what is causing those signals. Prediction errors update the hypothesis. Thus perception is “inside-out” as much as “outside-in.”

This is where Seth’s famous phrase “controlled hallucination” belongs. The phrase is often misunderstood. He is not saying that perception is arbitrary, fictional, or disconnected from reality. He is saying that all perception is generative: the brain actively produces perceptual worlds, and those worlds are continuously constrained by sensory input from the environment and the body. Ordinary perception differs from psychotic or psychedelic hallucination not because it is non-constructive, but because its construction is better controlled by sensory evidence and action.

The philosophical position here is a form of constructive realism. Experience does not give us direct, transparent access to the world as it is in itself. Yet experience is not mere illusion. It is a regulated, action-guiding model of a real world that pushes back.

4. Predictive processing as a philosophy of perception

Seth inherits and updates a long tradition running from Helmholtz’s unconscious inference through cybernetics, Bayesian brain theories, and contemporary predictive processing. In the Helmholtzian picture, perception is inference: the brain receives incomplete sensory signals and must infer their hidden causes. Seth’s version radicalizes this by suggesting that perceptual content is largely carried by top-down predictions, while bottom-up signals function as prediction errors.

On this view, the object you see is not built up from raw sensory atoms. It is inferred as the most plausible cause of sensory input under a generative model. The red cup on the table, the voice in the room, the bodily feeling of fatigue, and the felt presence of oneself are all predicted constructions. They are not invented out of nothing; they are constrained by environment, body, memory, attention, action, and prior expectation.

This makes Seth’s theory anti-naïve-realist. We do not perceive the world simply as it is. But it is also anti-idealist. The world is not dissolved into mind. The world and body are precisely what keep hallucination controlled. Perception is successful when the organism’s predictions are useful for action and regulation.

This model also explains why illusion and hallucination are not marginal curiosities but central evidence. If perception is predictive, then illusions reveal the machinery of normal perception under unusual conditions. Hallucination occurs when top-down predictions dominate inappropriately over sensory constraint. In everyday perception, the same generative machinery is at work, but kept in check.

The philosophical payoff is significant. Seth’s view weakens the sharp boundary between perception and imagination. Both involve generative modeling; they differ in degree and mode of constraint. Perception is imagination bound tightly to sensory error correction. Imagination is generative modeling loosened from immediate sensory correction. Hallucination is generative modeling insufficiently constrained by sensory input. Ordinary experience lies in the controlled middle.

5. The embodied self: “being a beast machine”

Seth’s most distinctive contribution is not just predictive perception. Predictive-processing accounts of perception are now widespread. His distinctive emphasis is that the conscious self is grounded in bodily regulation, especially interoception: the perception of the body from within.

In “Being a Beast Machine,” Seth and Manos Tsakiris conceptualize embodied selfhood as control-oriented predictive regulation, or allostasis, of physiological states. They argue that predictive perception generalizes from a fundamental biological imperative: maintaining physiological integrity, or staying alive. Perception of the world and perception of oneself happen “with, through, and because of” living bodies.

This is where Seth reverses Descartes. Descartes associated true mind with rational soul and treated animals as “beast machines.” Seth’s inversion is that we are not conscious despite being beast machines; our embodied selfhood arises because we are beast machines. The organism is not a container for consciousness. The living body is part of the explanatory basis of consciousness.

Interoception is central here. The brain must regulate blood pressure, heart rate, blood oxygenation, blood sugar, temperature, immune state, and other essential variables. It does this not merely by reflexive response but by predictive control. Seth and Friston’s active-interoceptive-inference work describes bodily states as regulated by autonomic reflexes shaped by descending predictions from deep generative models of the internal and external milieu.

This makes emotion philosophically central rather than secondary. In many older theories of mind, perception and cognition come first, while emotion is treated as an add-on. For Seth, affect and mood are fundamental forms of self-related experience because they concern the organism’s regulatory condition. Emotional valence—things going well or badly—is tied to the regulation of the living body. Seth’s recent AI essay explicitly links emotion, mood, and the basal feeling of being alive to perceptual predictions involved in controlling bodily condition.

The self, then, is not a hidden witness. It is a controlled hallucination of a special kind: a set of predictions about the body as object, the body as subject, and the body as a living condition of experience. The bodily self is relatively stable because the organism’s regulatory needs are continuous. Objects come and go, but the organism must continuously maintain itself. That continuity gives rise to the felt stability of “being me.”

6. The self is real, but not a thing

Seth’s view is anti-Cartesian but not eliminativist. The self is not an inner substance, soul, or spectator. Yet the self is not simply unreal. It is real as a process, model, and lived organization.

This matters philosophically. Some traditions respond to the constructed nature of selfhood by saying that the self is an illusion. Seth’s view is more subtle: the self is a perceptual construction, but perceptual constructions are not thereby worthless or false. The experienced self is like the experienced color of a surface. Color is not simply “out there” in the world independent of perceivers, but neither is it meaningless. It is a relation among world, body, brain, and action. Likewise, the self is not a metaphysical object hidden behind experience; it is the brain-body system’s model of itself as a subject of experience and agent of action.

The self has layers. Bodily selfhood concerns being and having a body. Perspectival selfhood concerns perceiving from a point of view. Volitional selfhood concerns intending and acting. Narrative selfhood concerns memory and personal continuity. Social selfhood concerns the way one’s identity is mediated by others. Seth’s hierarchy is not merely descriptive; it explains why disturbances of body, agency, memory, depersonalization, or social recognition can alter self-experience without eliminating consciousness altogether.

Agency follows the same pattern. The feeling of willing or causing an action is not a metaphysical proof of libertarian free will. It is a self-related perceptual inference concerning action, intention, and bodily control. Seth’s broader publication record includes work on intentional binding and Bayesian cue combination, consistent with the idea that agency is constructed from multiple cues rather than directly apprehended as an unanalyzable inner power.

A Sethian philosophy of agency would therefore be compatibilist or naturalistic, not libertarian. Actions are caused by brain-body-world dynamics, but the experience of agency remains functionally and phenomenologically real. It is part of how organisms regulate behavior, assign ownership, and maintain a coherent self-model.

7. Seth’s ontology: biological naturalism without dualism

Seth’s ontology is a form of naturalism. Consciousness is part of the natural world and must be explained by natural mechanisms. He rejects supernatural mind, Cartesian substance dualism, and the idea that consciousness floats free of biological embodiment.

But Seth’s naturalism is not straightforward computational functionalism. Classical computational functionalism says that mental states depend on functional or computational organization, not on the material substrate implementing that organization. In the strongest AI-friendly version, if a digital computer implements the right algorithm, consciousness follows. Seth has become increasingly skeptical of this assumption.

In his 2025 Behavioral and Brain Sciences target article, Seth explicitly challenges the assumption that computation is sufficient for consciousness and argues instead for a form of biological naturalism: consciousness depends on our nature as living organisms. He concludes that real artificial consciousness is unlikely along current AI trajectories but becomes more plausible as AI becomes more brain-like or life-like.

This biological naturalism is not simple “carbon chauvinism.” Seth does not say that only carbon-based biological organisms could ever be conscious. He says the material, dynamical, autopoietic, metabolic, and regulatory properties of living systems may matter. The question is not “can silicon run the right program?” but “what kinds of physical systems can instantiate the causal powers required for conscious self-regulation?”

This puts Seth near, but not identical to, John Searle’s biological naturalism. Like Searle, Seth resists the idea that syntax or computation alone is sufficient for consciousness. Unlike Searle’s more philosophical argument, Seth’s version is grounded in predictive processing, interoception, metabolism, allostasis, autopoiesis, and the free-energy/active-inference family of ideas.

He is also not a panpsychist. Seth criticizes panpsychism as the view that consciousness is fundamental and ubiquitous, and argues that its real problem is not that it sounds strange but that it explains nothing and generates no testable predictions. In this respect, he rejects both extremes: consciousness is not an inexplicable fundamental property everywhere, but neither is it a mere abstract computation that could appear wherever the right input-output function is implemented.

The best label is therefore: embodied biological predictive physicalism. It is physicalist in that consciousness is natural and materially realized. It is biological because living regulation matters. It is predictive because perception and selfhood are forms of generative inference. It is embodied because body and interoception are not optional add-ons. It is non-eliminativist because subjective experience is the explanandum, not something to be explained away.

8. Artificial consciousness in Seth’s work

Artificial consciousness is not peripheral to Seth’s thought. He wrote on “weak artificial consciousness” as early as 2009, distinguishing synthetic models that help illuminate consciousness from strong claims that machines literally instantiate it. In 2025–2026, artificial consciousness became central to his public and academic work through the Behavioral and Brain Sciences article “Conscious artificial intelligence and biological naturalism” and the Noema essay “The Mythology of Conscious AI.”

The first distinction Seth insists on is between intelligence and consciousness. Intelligence is about doing: achieving goals, solving problems, adapting behavior. Consciousness is about being: there being something it is like to exist as the system. AI systems can be intelligent in important senses while still lacking experience. Seth thinks people conflate the two because of anthropocentrism, human exceptionalism, and anthropomorphism: we take the human combination of intelligence and consciousness as definitive, then project it onto machines that display language or goal-directed behavior.

His second point is that conscious AI depends on a strong assumption: computational functionalism. The assumption is that implementing the right computation is sufficient for consciousness. Seth argues that this is often treated as obvious when it is actually a substantive and contestable metaphysical claim. If computational functionalism is false, standard digital AI may be intelligent without having any consciousness at all.

His critique of conscious AI has four main components.

First, brains are not computers in the relevant sense. Seth does not deny that computational models are useful. He denies that brains are cleanly separable into “software” and “hardware” in the way digital computers are. Biological brains are multiscale, metabolically active, chemically dense, self-maintaining systems. There is no sharp division between what brains do and what they are.

Second, mind may require dynamical processes that are not exhausted by algorithmic computation. Seth situates his view near dynamical-systems, embodied, enactive, embedded, and extended approaches, all of which resist treating mind as merely Turing-style symbol manipulation. He distinguishes broad functionalism from narrow computational functionalism: functional organization may matter, but the relevant organization may include biological dynamics, materiality, entropy, metabolism, and continuous regulation.

Third, life may matter. Seth’s biological naturalism says that properties of life may be necessary, though not necessarily sufficient, for consciousness. He explicitly admits that this is not a knock-down proof. But he argues it deserves attention because the agreed-upon candidates for consciousness are living, and because interoceptive self-regulation gives an explanatory bridge between life and experience.

Fourth, simulation is not instantiation. A digital simulation of digestion does not digest, and a simulation of rain does not make anything wet. Likewise, a simulation of a brain will instantiate consciousness only if consciousness is itself computational in the relevant sense. But that is precisely what Seth denies should be assumed.

His conclusion is cautious, not dogmatic. He argues that consciousness is unlikely simply to “come along for the ride” as AI becomes smarter, especially in standard silicon-based digital systems. But he does not rule out artificial consciousness altogether. He takes it more seriously for systems that are brain-like, life-like, neuromorphic, biological, or synthetic-biological; he even suggests that organoids may be more concerning than large language models.

Ethically, Seth distinguishes two dangers. The first is actually conscious AI: if machines can suffer, they may deserve moral consideration, perhaps even rights. The second is conscious-seeming AI: systems that are not conscious but persuade humans that they are. These can exploit psychological vulnerabilities, distort moral concern, and make us either overprotect machines or brutalize our own responses to apparent feeling. Seth’s current recommendation is therefore precautionary: nobody should deliberately try to create conscious AI, and society should also worry about machines designed to seem conscious.

9. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Seth’s position can be located by contrast with the major historical options.

Against Cartesian dualism, Seth denies that consciousness requires an immaterial soul or rational substance. His “beast machine” formulation deliberately reverses Descartes: embodied selfhood arises because we are living machines, not because we transcend mechanism.

Against behaviorism, Seth insists that consciousness is not merely behavior. The subjective properties of experience are real targets for explanation. His whole “real problem” framework depends on mapping first-person phenomenological properties to third-person biological mechanisms.

Against classical identity theory, Seth does not simply say that each conscious state is identical to some neural state in a one-to-one way. His approach is more dynamical and explanatory: experiences are mapped onto patterns of prediction, regulation, integration, embodiment, and control.

Against strong computational functionalism, Seth denies that the right abstract computation is obviously sufficient for consciousness. This is one of the major differences between his mature view and many AI-friendly theories of mind.

Against illusionism, Seth does not say that phenomenal consciousness is unreal. He says the self and world as experienced are constructed, but construction is not nonexistence. Consciousness is precisely what must be explained.

Against panpsychism, Seth argues that saying consciousness is fundamental and ubiquitous does not help empirical science unless it generates explanations and testable predictions.

Against pure global-workspace or access theories, Seth would likely say that cognitive availability and report are important but insufficient for explaining the phenomenological character of experience. His concern is not just which information becomes globally accessible, but why experience has its specific perceptual, affective, embodied, and self-related qualities.

Against integrated information theory, Seth is more ambivalent. He has worked on complexity and integrated information, but he resists treating consciousness as identical with a single abstract quantity, especially when such quantities become hard to measure in real systems. His early work already argued that no single measure captures the multidimensional complexity of conscious systems.

Historically, Seth is closest to a synthesis of Helmholtzian inference, cybernetics, active inference, 4E cognition, neurophenomenology, and biological naturalism. He inherits from Helmholtz the idea of perception as inference; from cybernetics the idea of regulation and control; from predictive processing the architecture of hierarchical prediction and prediction error; from phenomenology and neurophenomenology the seriousness of first-person structure; from embodied/enactive traditions the inseparability of mind from body and world; and from biological naturalism the thought that living material processes may matter for consciousness.

His historical significance is therefore not that he gives one final answer to the hard problem. It is that he reorients consciousness science around a tractable, phenomenology-sensitive, biologically grounded research program at precisely the moment when AI has revived old computational-functionalist assumptions.

10. A systematic philosophy of mind extracted from Seth

A comprehensive Sethian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as follows.

Ontology. The mind is not a separate substance. Consciousness is a natural, biological process realized by living systems capable of complex embodied self-regulation. It is physical, but not merely computational in the narrow digital-algorithmic sense.

Explanation. Consciousness should be explained by mapping phenomenological properties to biological mechanisms. The correct unit of explanation is not just the brain in isolation but the brain-body-world system, especially where selfhood and affect are concerned.

Perception. Perception is controlled hallucination: a generative, predictive construction constrained by sensory evidence. The world we experience is not passively copied; it is actively inferred.

Reality. Seth’s view is constructivist but realist. Perception is constructed, but it is controlled by causal interaction with a real world and a real body. The fact that perception is model-based does not make the world unreal.

Self. The self is not an inner observer. It is a layered self-model generated by predictive processes. Bodily selfhood is basic; narrative and social selves are higher-level constructions. The self is real as a process, not as a metaphysical object.

Body. The body is not peripheral to mind. Interoception, allostasis, metabolism, and physiological regulation are central to consciousness, especially to affect, mood, and the sense of being alive.

Emotion. Emotion is not noise added to cognition. It is a core mode of interoceptive inference and bodily regulation. Valence reflects the organism’s condition relative to its needs and viability.

Agency. Agency is constructed through predictive control of action and self-related inference. The sense of will is real as an experience and function, but not evidence of a nonphysical inner cause.

Animal minds. Seth’s position is naturally anti-human-exceptionalist. Since consciousness is tied to embodied biological regulation rather than language or human-level intelligence, non-human animals are serious candidates for consciousness. His publication record includes work on mammalian, non-mammalian, and beyond-human consciousness.

Artificial minds. Intelligence and consciousness can dissociate. Current AI may be increasingly intelligent without being conscious. Artificial consciousness is not impossible in principle, but it likely requires more than abstract computation: perhaps life-like, brain-like, embodied, self-maintaining dynamics.

Ethics. Moral status is closely tied to the capacity for conscious experience, especially suffering. This makes animal consciousness ethically urgent and AI consciousness conceptually dangerous. Conscious-seeming AI also creates ethical risks even if it is not actually conscious.

The result is a philosophy in which mind is neither ghost nor machine-program. It is organismic world-modeling from the inside of a living body.

11. Critical assessment

Seth’s program has several major strengths. It treats consciousness as real without surrendering to mystery. It respects phenomenology without abandoning neuroscience. It explains why perception, hallucination, bodily selfhood, emotion, and agency belong to the same theoretical space. It also offers a serious corrective to the current tendency to infer consciousness from linguistic fluency or intelligence in AI.

The main philosophical vulnerability is that the “real problem” may be accused of sidestepping rather than solving the hard problem. Even if Seth explains why red experience, pain, bodily presence, or agency have their particular structures, one might still ask why those biological mechanisms are accompanied by any experience at all. Seth’s likely reply is that this residual question may lose force as mechanistic explanations accumulate, but that is a promissory methodological claim, not a deductive refutation of Chalmers.

A second vulnerability is the boundary problem for biological naturalism. If life matters, what exactly about life matters? Metabolism? Autopoiesis? Homeostasis? Interoception? Nervous systems? Active inference? Could plants be conscious? Could organoids? Could synthetic biological systems? Could a non-biological system instantiate the relevant dynamics? Seth acknowledges that biological naturalism is not yet proven and remains a minority view.

A third issue is that predictive processing may be too general. Prediction-error minimization occurs across many biological and perhaps artificial systems, but not all such systems are conscious. Seth therefore needs additional constraints: complexity, integration, embodiment, interoception, self-regulation, perhaps reportability in humans, perhaps specific cortical dynamics. His own anti-monolithic stance helps here, but it also means the theory is not yet a simple criterion for consciousness.

A fourth issue concerns exteroceptive qualia. Interoceptive inference gives a powerful account of selfhood, mood, emotion, and the feeling of being alive. But the specific qualitative character of colors, sounds, smells, and abstract thoughts still requires detailed mapping. Seth’s predictive framework can address these, but the interoceptive “beast machine” account is strongest for selfhood and affect.

A fifth issue is measurement. Seth’s early work is careful about this: some aspects of consciousness are measurable, some are resistant to quantification, and no single measure suffices. This is philosophically mature, but it also means consciousness science will remain plural, theory-laden, and methodologically difficult.

Despite these challenges, Seth’s view is one of the most coherent contemporary attempts to build a philosophy of mind out of cognitive neuroscience without erasing subjectivity.

Final synthesis

Anil Seth’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence:

Consciousness is the lived, affectively valenced, self-involving world-model generated by a living body-brain system as it predicts and controls its own embodied existence.

Its historical role is to move the field beyond three unsatisfactory poles: Cartesian dualism, which separates mind from body; reductive computationalism, which abstracts mind away from biological materiality; and panpsychism, which makes consciousness ubiquitous but empirically inert. Seth’s alternative is a biological, embodied, predictive naturalism: consciousness is not magic, not mere information processing, and not an illusion. It is what self-regulating life feels like from the inside when it models a world and itself within that world.
